audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Leviticus / Vayiqra childbirth purification text tradition, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for childbirth blood/purification, male/female birth durations, circumcision on eighth day, offerings, and poverty provision. Strong anti-body-shame safeguards required.

verses.restored: Speak to children of Israel, saying: When a woman conceives seed and bears a male, she shall be unclean seven days; as in days of impurity of her menstruation she shall be unclean.
On eighth day flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised.
Thirty-three days she shall remain in bloods of purification; she shall not touch any holy thing and shall not come into sanctuary until days of her purification are fulfilled.
If she bears a female, she shall be unclean two weeks as in her menstruation, and sixty-six days she shall remain over bloods of purification.

When days of her purification are fulfilled, for son or for daughter, she shall bring lamb, son of a year, for burnt offering, and son of pigeon or turtledove for sin/purification offering to entrance of tent of meeting, to priest.
He shall bring it near before YHWH and make atonement/covering for her, and she shall be clean from flow/source of her bloods. This is Torah/instruction for one giving birth, for male or female.

If her hand does not find enough for lamb, she shall take two turtledoves or two sons of pigeon, one for burnt offering and one for sin/purification offering; priest shall make atonement/covering for her, and she shall be clean.

translation_notes: Childbirth impurity is ritual-state language tied to blood/life and sanctuary approach, not moral fault, sexual shame, or disgust toward mother or baby.
Bloods of purification should be preserved; it does not mean the mother sinned by giving birth.
Different durations for male/female birth require historical/source and gender-ethics audit; do not explain away too quickly.
Bird offering option protects access for poor mothers.
safety_note: Do not use Leviticus 12 to shame pregnancy, birth, menstruation, postpartum bodies, women, girls, or mothers. The chapter is ancient ritual-state instruction around birth and sanctuary timing, with an accessible offering for poverty.
